graph_vector_fulltext.message:
Along the AI value chain, multiple parties may supply tools, services, components, or processes that the provider incorporates into an AI system for purposes like training, testing/evaluation, retraining, or integration into software. As a baseline, the provider that incorporates those inputs remains responsible and “should… ensure that the AI system embedded in the final product complies with the requirements of this Regulation,” including having responsibility for conformity assessment.

For component suppliers, the open-source exception limits upstream obligations: third parties that make accessible to the public tools, services, processes, or AI components (other than general-purpose AI models) under a free and open-source licence “should not be mandated to comply with requirements targeting the responsibilities along the AI value chain,” particularly vis‑à‑vis the provider that integrates them. Even so, developers of such free/open-source components are encouraged (not required) to use widely adopted documentation practices (e.g., model cards and data sheets) to accelerate information sharing along the value chain.

This exception is framed as applying to tools/services/processes/components other than general-purpose AI models, while the Regulation separately imposes obligations on “providers of general-purpose AI models” (including for models that may present systemic risks), with obligations applying when those models are placed on the market. In other words, the open-source carve-out described here does not remove the Regulation’s separate obligation regime for providers of general-purpose AI models.

Putting it together: open-source component suppliers (non‑GPAI) are generally not forced into value-chain responsibility requirements, GPAI model providers have their own dedicated obligation set, and the downstream provider integrating everything into the final AI system retains the ultimate duty to ensure compliance of the final system/product under the Regulation.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: Along the AI value chain, multiple parties supply components. How do the obligations for component suppliers interact with the open-source exception, the GPAI model provider obligations, and the provider's ultimate responsibility?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
Along the AI value chain, multiple parties may supply tools, services, components, or processes that the provider incorporates into an AI system for purposes like training, testing/evaluation, retraining, or integration into software. As a baseline, the provider that incorporates those inputs remains responsible and “should… ensure that the AI system embedded in the final product complies with the requirements of this Regulation,” including having responsibility for conformity assessment.

For component suppliers, the open-source exception limits upstream obligations: third parties that make accessible to the public tools, services, processes, or AI components (other than general-purpose AI models) under a free and open-source licence “should not be mandated to comply with requirements targeting the responsibilities along the AI value chain,” particularly vis‑à‑vis the provider that integrates them. Even so, developers of such free/open-source components are encouraged (not required) to use widely adopted documentation practices (e.g., model cards and data sheets) to accelerate information sharing along the value chain.

This exception is framed as applying to tools/services/processes/components other than general-purpose AI models, while the Regulation separately imposes obligations on “providers of general-purpose AI models” (including for models that may present systemic risks), with obligations applying when those models are placed on the market. In other words, the open-source carve-out described here does not remove the Regulation’s separate obligation regime for providers of general-purpose AI models.

Putting it together: open-source component suppliers (non‑GPAI) are generally not forced into value-chain responsibility requirements, GPAI model providers have their own dedicated obligation set, and the downstream provider integrating everything into the final AI system retains the ultimate duty to ensure compliance of the final system/product under the Regulation.

 based on the generally acknowledged state of the art, in order to enable the provider to fully comply with the  obligations set out in this Regulation, without compromising their own intellectual property rights or trade secrets. (89) Third parties making accessible to the public tools, services, processes, or AI components other than  general-purpose AI models, should not be mandated to comply with requirements targeting the responsibilities  along the AI value chain, in particular towards the provider that has used or integrated them, when those
----
 along the AI value chain, in particular towards the provider that has used or integrated them, when those tools,  services, processes, or AI components are made accessible under a free and open-source licence. Developers of free  and open-source tools, services, processes, or AI components other than general-purpose AI models should be  encouraged to implement widely adopted documentation practices, such as model cards and data sheets, as a way to  accelerate information sharing along the AI value chain, allowing
----
 obligations of the provider established in this Regulation and should, in particular, ensure that the AI system  embedded in the final product complies with the requirements of this Regulation. (88) Along the AI value chain multiple parties often supply AI systems, tools and services but also components or  processes that are incorporated by the provider into the AI system with various objectives, including the model  training, model retraining, model testing and evaluation, integration into software, or other aspects of model  development. Those
----
published and assessed as suitable to cover the relevant obligations by the AI Office, compliance with a European  harmonised standard should grant providers the presumption of conformity. Providers of general-purpose AI  models should furthermore be able to demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means, if codes of practice  or harmonised standards are not available, or they choose not to rely on those. (118) This Regulation regulates AI systems and AI models by imposing certain requirements and obligations for relevant  market actors that are placing them

chunks.text: based on the generally acknowledged state of the art, in order to enable the provider to fully comply with the obligations set out in this Regulation, without compromising their own intellectual property rights or trade secrets. (89) Third parties making accessible to the public tools, services, processes, or AI components other than general-purpose AI models, should not be mandated to comply with requirements targeting the responsibilities along the AI value chain, in particular towards the provider that has used or integrated them, when those

along the AI value chain, in particular towards the provider that has used or integrated them, when those tools, services, processes, or AI components are made accessible under a free and open-source licence. Developers of free and open-source tools, services, processes, or AI components other than general-purpose AI models should be encouraged to implement widely adopted documentation practices, such as model cards and data sheets, as a way to accelerate information sharing along the AI value chain, allowing

obligations of the provider established in this Regulation and should, in particular, ensure that the AI system embedded in the final product complies with the requirements of this Regulation. (88) Along the AI value chain multiple parties often supply AI systems, tools and services but also components or processes that are incorporated by the provider into the AI system with various objectives, including the model training, model retraining, model testing and evaluation, integration into software, or other aspects of model development. Those

published and assessed as suitable to cover the relevant obligations by the AI Office, compliance with a European harmonised standard should grant providers the presumption of conformity. Providers of general-purpose AI models should furthermore be able to demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means, if codes of practice or harmonised standards are not available, or they choose not to rely on those. (118) This Regulation regulates AI systems and AI models by imposing certain requirements and obligations for relevant market actors that are placing them
